Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 1

'Wa' (By) is used for an oath. The oaths sworn by Allah Almighty in the Holy Qur'an are not meant to glorify the objects sworn by, but rather to present them as witnesses to the statement being sworn to (the maqsam 'alayh). The mentor-scholar (ra) has discussed this issue in detail in his treatise *Al-Im'an fi Aqsam al-Qur'an*. Its Urdu translation by our pen has also been published. Those who seek details should read it. In the light of this meaning of the oath, if 'Was-Saffati Saffa' is translated as: 'The angels who remain present, standing in rows, are witnesses,' it will entirely and precisely convey the essence of the oath.

'Saffat' (those standing in rows) is used here as an attribute of the angels, and this is explained in this very Surah itself through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel (peace be upon him), whose statement is quoted: 'And there is not among us any except that he has a known position. And indeed, we are those who stand in rows. And indeed, we are those who exalt [Allah].' (164-166).

The context indicates that this refers to those angels who belong to the assembly of the exalted hosts (Al-Mala' al-A'la) and remain standing in rows around the Throne of God. Their mention has occurred in Surah Az-Zumar in these words: 'And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne on all sides, exalting [Allah] with praise of their Lord' (75).

Ayah 2

'Zajr' means to drive away, scold, and repulse. This describes the second attribute of these very angels—that if the devils try to approach the exalted assembly (Al-Mala' al-A'la), they drive them away. To keep the devils away from the exalted assembly, Allah Almighty has also made a special arrangement, the details of which are coming below.

Ayah 3

This is the third attribute of these angels. By the 'recitation of the message' (Taliyati Dhikra), it is meant that very same act which has been described in Surah Az-Zumar with the words: 'exalting [Allah] with praise of their Lord' and in this Surah with the words: 'And indeed, we are those who exalt [Allah].' That is, they perform the praise and glorification of Allah Almighty.

Here, the rule of the Arabic language should be kept in view: when the description of attributes is conjoined with the particle 'Fa' (then) as it is here, it serves as a proof of two things. First, it proves that all these attributes qualify the same entity; therefore, the view of those who have declared different qualified entities for these attributes is, in our view, contrary to Arabic usage. Second, it proves that there is a gradual progression in these attributes. Regarding the interpretation we have made, the first point is entirely clear: all these attributes belong to the angels. As for the second point, if you reflect, you will find that there is a progression in these attributes exactly like the progression in our prayers. Just as we stand in rows before God, then seek refuge from Satan, and then perform the praise and glorification of our Lord, similarly, the angels stand in rows around the Throne of God, then drive away the devils, and then perform the praise and glorification of their Lord.

Ayah 4

This is the statement sworn to (the maqsam 'alayh) of this oath. After presenting these angels' worship and their praise and glorification as witnesses, He states: 'Indeed, your Lord is One.' From this, it emerged that those who have believed the angels to be the daughters of God, associated them in His divinity, and are worshipping them on the hope of their intercession, are caught in sheer foolishness. The angels' own conduct is an open refutation of these foolish people. This is because they are continuously active in the worship, praise, and glorification of God, while these foolish people have made them partners of God and are busy in their worship.

Ayah 5

This is a subsequent predicate (khabar) after the first. That is, that same One God is the Lord of all the heavens and the earth and all that is between them, and He is the Lord of all the points of sunrise. The meaning is: let no one remain in the misconception that He has entrusted the management of the distant corners of His endless kingdom to His other partners. He stands in no need of anyone's help. He Himself is the Master of every corner and angle of His universe, and He Himself manages it under His commands. If the angels have any role in this universe, it is not as partners of God, but as His obedient ambassadors and agents. Whatever they do, they do in compliance with the commands of their Lord.

In 'Rabbul-Mashariq' (Lord of the points of sunrise), the plural 'Mashariq' is used in view of the vastness of its horizons. We have explained under the verse of Surah Al-A'raf that the plural is sometimes used to express the vastness and length of something. In the Qur'an, 'Mashariq' (points of sunrise) and 'Magharib' (points of sunset) have occurred in this very sense. Similarly, where the objective is to point to both ends of something, they sometimes bring it in the dual form. Thus, 'Mashriqayn' and 'Maghribayn' are also used in the Qur'an. After 'Rabbul-Mashariq', 'Rabbul-Magharib' (Lord of the points of sunset) is omitted due to contextual clarity. This is because the points of sunset are subordinate to the points of sunrise. When the primary is mentioned, the secondary is mentioned, as it were, automatically. In some places, it has also been stated explicitly. For example: 'So I swear by the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets that indeed We are competent' (Al-Ma'arij: 40). The reason for specifically mentioning the points of sunrise with special care can also be that in the world, the polytheists have mostly worshipped the sun, which rises from the east.

Ayah 6-7

In the preceding verse, the angels were mentioned—showing that although foolish people have given them the status of divinity, the state of even those in the exalted assembly (Al-Mala' al-A'la) is that they remain continuously standing in rows before their Lord, active in His praise and glorification. Now, in the subsequent verses, the devils from among the Jinn are mentioned—showing that foolish people have assumed regarding them that their reach is up to the exalted assembly and that they obtain news of the unseen from there; thus, on this hope, they are worshipped as a source of obtaining the knowledge of the unseen. However, no one has access to the exalted assembly. If any rebellious devil tries to reach there, he is struck by a piercing flame.

'Hifdhan' (protection) is for the emphasis of an omitted verb—meaning: 'Wa hifdhan-ha hifdhan' (And We protected it with a thorough protection). For this reason, its meaning is that We have thoroughly protected the sky from the intrusion of the devils. This shows that the stars are, on one hand, the adornment of the lowest sky, and on the other hand, nature uses them to reprimand those devils who try to reach the exalted assembly. This theme is expressed in the Qur'an in various styles. For example: 'And We have certainly beautified the nearest heaven with lamps and have made them [as projectiles] for stoning devils' (Al-Mulk: 5). In another place: 'And We have placed within the heaven great stars and have beautified it for the observers. And We have protected it from every devil expelled [from Our mercy] - Except one who steals a hearing and is pursued by a clear burning flame.' (Al-Hijr: 16-18).

"They may not listen to the exalted assembly and are pelted from every side, Repelled, and for them is a constant punishment." (8-9)

'They may not listen...'—this negation of the verb is in the sense of the negation of the benefit of the verb. That is: the devils do try to listen to the exalted assembly, but they do not get to listen. When they wish to do so, they are pelted from every side. 'Duhoor' means to drive away and repulse, and 'wasib' means constant. That is: in this world, they will remain cursed and stoned in this manner, and in the Hereafter, they will face a constant punishment.

Ayah 10

That is: after this firm arrangement of nature, there is no possibility that any devil can reach the exalted assembly or listen to their words. If any rebellious devil can do anything, he can at most try to snatch some word like snatchers. So, to block this, this arrangement is present: a burning flame pursues him from the celestial towers.

This detail clarifies that neither do the angels have any share in God's divinity, nor do the Jinn have access to the exalted assembly to find out any news of the unseen from there. For this reason, those who are worshipping the angels, believing them to be the daughters of God, are foolish, and those who are trying to establish contact and mediation with the Jinn, believing them to be a means of obtaining the knowledge of the unseen, are also foolish. It should be kept in mind here that the polytheism of the Arabs and all the popularity of their fortune-telling were based on these two very concepts. The Qur'an has ended this entire business of their deviation by clarifying the reality of both the Jinn and the angels here.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 11–74

Next are the details of the events of the Resurrection, and the objective is to show what state the polytheists and their deities will face on the Day of Judgment—how the leaders of polytheism and their followers will curse one another! And how Allah Almighty will bless the believers, who remain firm on the belief of Tawhid and Resurrection despite all opposition, with the great success. Recite the verses.

An Obvious Proof for the Resurrection

'Then inquire of them, "Are they a stronger creation or those We have created?" Indeed, We created them from sticky clay.' (11)

To describe the events of the Resurrection, first the question of its possibility is taken up as a preface—stating that if these people deny your warnings of the Resurrection because being raised alive again after death is improbable in their view, then ask them: is it more difficult to create these heavens and the earth, these oceans and mountains, these sun and moon which God has created, or humans? The God Who is competent to bring these great things into existence from non-existence, why would He be unable to recreate a contemptible thing like a human? This same theme is expressed in Surah Ghafir: 'The creation of the heavens and the earth is greater than the creation of mankind, but most of the people do not know.' (57). This same question is raised in another place: 'Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it.' (An-Nazi'at: 27).

'Indeed, We created them from sticky clay': 'Teen lazib' means sticky clay. The meaning is: We created them from sticky clay. We did not have to manage any special resources to create them, such that managing them again would be difficult for Us. The very clay from which We created them is present with Us in abundance. We will create them again whenever We will. When We faced no difficulty the first time, why would this task be difficult for Us again!

Here, in 'or those We have created' (am man khalaqna), by the usage of 'man' (who/whom), the mind also goes toward the fact that the comparison is being made with the creation of the angels and the Jinn. In this case, the meaning would be: ask them whether it is more difficult to create them or the other creations We have created (meaning the Jinn and the angels). We created them from dust, and the angels and Jinn from light and fire; so if We were competent to create those, why would We be unable to recreate humans!

'But you wonder, while they mock.' (12)

A Great Distance Between the Speaker and the Addressees

That is: for you, their denial is a matter of wonder, and for them, your claim is a matter of mockery. You are wondering how any sensible person can deny such an obvious reality, and their state is that they are mocking—wondering how anyone possessing intellect and senses can say such a thing! The meaning is: there is a great distance (after-ul-mashriqayn) between you and them, and you are not forming a correct estimate of this distance, because of which their attitude is a cause of worry for you.

'And when they are reminded, they do not remember.' (13)

This is a description of the logical consequence of this mental distance—stating that when their way of thinking is so different that what is self-evident to you is a joke to them, then all your warning and reminding is entirely useless for them. Whatever advice is given to them flies away in the wind; no proof can affect them.

'And when they see a sign, they mock And say, "This is not but obvious magic."' (14-15)

It states that such people are not convinced even by the greatest miracle. To harass you, they demand it, but when any sign comes before them, they mock it and say: 'This is only obvious magic.'

"'When we have died and become dust and bones, are we indeed to be resurrected? Or our forefathers of old?'" (16-17)

This is an expression of their mockery. It states: they say: 'When we die and decay into dust and bones, will we be raised alive after that? And their wonder of wonders is: will our ancestors also be raised!' These things appear entirely far from reason to them, because of which they believe that those who warn them of this are crazy.

'Say, "Yes, and you will be [rendered] contemptible."' (18)

Since their question was by way of mockery, the reply is also given in a highly sharp style. It states: 'Yes, you will be raised, and at that time, you will be rendered highly contemptible!'

"It will be only one shout, and at once they will be staring." (19)

The Panic of the Deniers at the Manifestation of the Resurrection

That is, these people should not remain in the misconception that to raise them again Allah Almighty will have to make some special arrangements. This task will be performed in a single shout. In a single shout, they will stand up and stare in panic. This same theme is expressed in Surah An-Nazi'at: 'It will be only one shout, And at once they will be alert [on the earth's surface].' (13-14). The panic and terror in this style of expression need no explanation.

"And they will say, 'O woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense.'" (20)

That is, the Resurrection which they are denying today with such arrogance, they will cry out at that time: 'O woe to us! This is indeed that Day of Recompense which we continuously denied.'

"[It will be said], 'This is the Day of Judgement which you used to deny.'" (21)

Upon this statement of theirs, the angels will say: 'Yes! This is indeed that Day of Judgment which you continuously denied!'

"[The angels will be ordered], 'Gather those who committed injustice and their companions and what they used to worship Besides Allah, and guide them to the path of Hell.'" (22-23)

'Those who committed injustice' (alladhina zalamu) refers to the polytheists, because the greatest injustice is polytheism. Here, the context is proof that the allusion is specifically to the leaders of polytheism.

'and their companions' (wa azwajahum): 'Azwaj' here is used in the sense of associates, followers, and co-religionists.

'and what they used to worship' refers to their partners from among the Jinn and those idols and stones they worshipped.

On the Day of Judgment, regarding all of them, the angels will be commanded by Allah: 'Gather all of them and show them the way to Hell.'

"And stop them; indeed, they are to be questioned. [They will be asked], 'What is [wrong] with you why do you not help each other?'" (24-25)

This is a depiction of their disgrace and helplessness on that Day.

'What is [wrong] with you why do you not help each other?': This is what they will be stopped to be asked. It will be said: 'Why is it that you are not helping one another today?' In the world, you carried one another's banners, but today neither are the leaders helping the followers, nor are the followers helping the leaders; neither are the deities seen doing anything for the worshippers, nor are the worshippers expressing any devotion to the deities. Rather, everyone is in a state of self-concern. The time for true devotion to one another has arrived now, but at this time, everyone is concerned only with themselves!

"But they, this Day, are surrendering." (26)

'Istislam' means to surrender and become obedient. It states: today these people are highly obedient and humble! Until yesterday, their arrogance was such that they did not let anyone touch their shoulder, but today all have laid down their weapons!

"And they will approach one another, questioning each other." (27)

'Tasazul' (questioning) is in the sense of mutual questioning and answering, and the detail of this bickering which will occur on that Day between the leaders and their followers is coming.

"They will say, 'Indeed, you used to come to us from the right.' They will say, 'Rather, you [yourselves] were not believers.'" (28-29)

The context is proof that the subject of the first 'qalu' (they said) is the common people, and of the second is their leaders. That is: on that Day, the common people will say to their leaders: 'This is the misfortune brought by you. You continuously surrounded us from our right and left so that we would not listen to the Prophet's word. If you had not placed obstacles in our way, we would have certainly believed.' The leaders will immediately cut their statement and reply to them: 'Your accusation is entirely false; you yourselves were not ready to believe.'

"And we had over you no authority; rather, you were a transgressing people." (30)

'Sultan' means power and authority. That is: 'We had no power or authority over your hearts that you are placing your blame on us; you were yourselves disobedient and rebellious people that after the truth had come before you clearly, you followed our word instead of following it.'

A Rare Style

Our commentators faced great difficulty in interpreting the clause: 'Indeed, you used to come to us from the right' (Innakum kuntum ta'toonana 'anil-yameen). The reason is that they could not understand the rare style of discourse observed here. In the Qur'an, in the description of the mutual bickering of the people of Hell, this eloquent style is used in various places—that as soon as the speaker begins his statement, the addressee cuts it to reply, so that they may save themselves from the blame being laid on them. We have mentioned some examples of this in the previous Surahs. Highly eloquent examples of this will also follow, particularly in the Surahs of the final group.

Here too, the same style is observed. As soon as the common people want to blame their leaders, and the words come out of their mouths: 'Indeed, you used to come to us from the right...', their leaders understand that they want to say: 'You used to come to us from our right and left to stop us from listening to the Prophet's word'; so as soon as the words 'from the right' come out of their mouths, they immediately anticipate them, cut their statement, and begin their defense. To express this intervention and cutting of the statement, the Qur'an has ended the statement on the words 'from the right' ('anil-yameen) so that from the style of discourse itself, the reality is clarified: those who are blindly following their leaders today, a Day will come when their leaders will not let them even complete their statement, but will cut it midway to declare their disassociation. Under verses 31–33 of Surah Saba, the detail of the bickering of the leaders and common people has passed. Have a look at it.

"So the word of our Lord has come into effect upon us; indeed, we will taste [punishment]. And we led you astray; indeed, we were [ourselves] astray." (31-32)

This is also quoted from the statement of the leaders—stating: 'Now nothing is to be gained from blaming one another. We are criminals and you are also criminals; therefore, that word of our Lord which He uttered in reply to Iblis's challenge—stating: "I will fill Hell with you and those who follow you"—has come into effect upon both of us. Now we must necessarily taste the punishment which is destined for criminals like us. "And we led you astray; indeed, we were [ourselves] astray"—meaning: we would be subject to blame only if we ourselves had been righteous and tried to make you bad. Whatever we were ourselves, we tried to make you. It was your own lack of insight that you followed us, and a greater lack of insight is that you are blaming us for the end of our following! Why did you expect to eat apples from a colocynth (indrayan) plant?'

"So indeed they, that Day, in the punishment will be sharing." (33)

Both Leaders and Followers in Hell

The meaning is that on that Day, the excuse of the common people—that others were responsible for their deviation—will be of no use. Allah Almighty has endowed every person with enough intellect to distinguish between truth and falsehood, particularly when those who invite to the truth are also present. For this reason, such common people and such leaders will both share in the punishment.

"Indeed, that is how We deal with the criminals. Indeed, they, when it was said to them, 'There is no deity but Allah,' were arrogant And were saying, 'Are we to leave our gods for a mad poet?'" (34-36)

The Fate of the Criminals of the Quraysh

This makes the discourse relevant to the present situation—stating: this is not merely the story of others, but Allah Almighty's dealing with all criminals will be the same. Their crime was that when they were invited to: 'There is no deity but Allah,' they showed arrogance and said: 'Are we to leave our deities at the word of a crazy poet!' When this same thing is said by the people of today to their messenger, why would they not face the same situation? Allah Almighty's dealing with all criminals is the same.

"Rather, he has brought the truth and confirmed the messengers." (37)

Reply to the Quraysh

This is a reply to the Quraysh—stating that the person who is inviting you to Tawhid and warning you of God's punishment is neither crazy nor a poet; rather, he is informing you of an inevitable reality. A highly clear proof of his being a messenger of truth is that all messengers from Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) to Hazrat Masih (Jesus) prophesied regarding him, and with his advent, their prophecies and teachings have been confirmed. This person is not an alien among you, nor are his teachings strange. His news was given by previous messengers, and his teachings are confirmed by previous scriptures. If you are unaware of this, ask the People of the Book; those who are honest among them will confirm this. These matters have been discussed in detail in the previous Surahs, so we limit our discussion to an allusion here.

"Indeed, you will taste the painful punishment, And you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do." (38-39)

That is, do not impute the punishment of which Allah's messenger is informing you to some madness; rather, it is a reality. If you deny him, you will necessarily face this painful punishment, and this will not be any injustice to you, but will be exactly the recompense of your deeds appearing before you.

"Except the chosen servants of Allah." (40)

Those Safe from the Punishment

The exception is disconnected (munqati'). The meaning is: everyone is going to face this end. Only those servants of Allah will remain safe from it whom Allah has chosen for His mercy. We have been clarifying in various places that Allah Almighty chooses for His favor and mercy only those servants who establish their entitlement through their faith and deeds. The word 'mukhlaseen' (chosen/sincere) is used specifically to show the reality that whatever anyone receives, they will receive only through the favor and mercy of God; no one else has the power to give anything to anyone, as the polytheists have assumed.

"Those will have a known provision." (41)

The Meaning of Known Provision

It states that indeed these are the ones for whom there will be a known and familiar provision with God. The meaning is: the program of times for their eating and drinking will be appointed according to their desire, and the things presented before them will also be in accordance with their liking and selection. The condition of the people of Hell will be that the keepers of Hell will throw something before them whenever they please, and whatever they throw will be entirely alien and highly bad, but whatever is presented before the people of Paradise will be presented in accordance with their demand, their liking, and their program. Those things will not be alien to them such that they feel aversion or panic upon seeing them, but every single thing will be known and familiar, though the standard of everything will be that which is specified for Paradise and its people. 'Every time they are provided with a portion of fruit therefrom, they will say, "This is what we were provided with before." And they were given things in resemblance.' (Al-Baqarah: 25). To understand this verse, have a look at what we wrote under it.

"Fruits; and they will be honored In the Gardens of Pleasure, On thrones facing each other." (42-44)

That is, fruits will be presented before them, and they will be dealt with with great honor and respect. Regarding the people of Hell, it has passed in verse 18 that they will be packed into Hell with great disgrace, and below in verses 62–67, it is coming that Zaqqum will be given to them for food and boiling water for drink. On the contrary, the people of Paradise will receive fruits for food, and will be dealt with with great respect.

'In the Gardens of Pleasure' points to their permanent station—showing that they will be in the gardens of pleasure; therefore, they will enjoy all the blessings they desire and in whatever quantity they desire.

The Mutual Love and Joy of the People of Paradise

'On thrones facing each other' expresses their mutual love and joy—showing that they will sit on thrones facing each other. It should be clear that when there is resentment and malice in hearts, people sit turning their faces away from one another. Since the hearts of the people of Paradise will be entirely clean, they will sit facing one another. Above, the detail of the bickering of the people of Hell has passed. In contrast to that, this is the depiction of the people of Paradise.

"There will be circulated among them a cup [of wine] from a flowing spring, White and delicious to the drinkers, No bad effect is there in it, nor from it will they be intoxicated." (45-47)

The Wine of Paradise

'Ka's' in Arabic is used for the vessel and also for what is in it, i.e., the cup and the wine. 'Ma'een' means pure and unmixed; in verse 30 of Surah Al-Mulk, a parallel is present for this meaning. 'Ghawl' means to destroy secretly, and from this, it is also used for any hidden harm. 'Yunzifoon' is used in the sense of being exhausted and also in the sense of the suspension of intellect; here, both meanings are suitable. The reaction of wine is manifest in the form of a hangover, body aches, and extreme exhaustion, and also in the form of the suspension of intellect—the first is physical pain, and the second is moral corruption. The wine of the people of Paradise will be free from both of these calamities. The meaning of the verses is: cups of pure wine will be circulated among them; this wine will be free from every impurity, highly clear and transparent, and pure pleasure and joy for the drinkers; there will be no hidden harm in it, nor will the intellect be suspended by drinking it... meaning it will possess all the virtues which the highest quality wine must possess, and at the same time, it will be free from every evil which is necessarily present in even the best wine of this world.

"And with them will be women limiting [their] glances, with large, beautiful eyes, As if they were delicate eggs [closely] guarded." (48-49)

The Houris of Paradise

No pleasure or joy of man is complete without woman; therefore, after the blessings of Paradise, this mentions those Houris who will be given to the people of Paradise. You observed that in the description of Paradise, the specific taste of the Arabs is considered, because they were the primary addressees of the Qur'an; similarly, in the description of the Houris, the same words of comparison and description are used which the Arab poets used for virgins and beauties. 'Qasirat-ut-Tarf' primarily means those who limit their glances, which is an expression of their modesty. The Arabs considered modesty to be the greatest beauty of a woman. 'Een' is the plural of 'a'yan'—meaning they will have large, beautiful eyes, like those of a gazelle.

'As if they were delicate eggs [closely] guarded': 'Baydhun maktun' refers to the eggs of an ostrich. In Arabic poetry, comparison of beauties with the eggs of an ostrich is found in abundance, and reflecting on it, it emerges that in this comparison they consider modesty, protection, and color. 'Maknoon' points to their being untouched. It should be kept in mind here that just as the Arabs were highly sensitive and jealous regarding the protection of their virgins, the ostrich also dedicates its life to protect its eggs. In this comparison, golden-white eggs are meant. The Arab poets mention the golden-white color of beauties extensively. For a beautiful woman, 'Al-Safra' (the golden) is a well-known attribute among them. I can present references of Arabic poetry in support of all these matters, but I want to avoid unnecessary length.

"And they will approach one another, inquiring of each other." (50)

A Conversation of the People of Paradise

Now, next up to verse 59, is a conversation of the people of Paradise which will occur among them when they are sitting in Paradise reclining on thrones. From this conversation, an estimate can be formed of the joy they will experience over the successes they achieved in facing the oppositions for the sake of the truth in the world, and of the self-reliance that will overcome them from the realization that now they have achieved permanent release from the fears of death and punishment.

One of the companions of Paradise will relate a story of his past to his companions sitting near him—stating: 'I had a companion who used to ask me with great wonder: "Are you indeed among those who believe in the Resurrection? Is this acceptable to your intellect—that when we die and decay into dust and bones, we will be raised again to face the reward and punishment of our deeds?"'

"He will say, 'Would you [care to] look?'" After this, he will say to his companions: 'Let us see what happened to him!' This style of expression in Arabic is used to incite someone to a task, just as we say: 'Just find out! Just have a look!' The meaning is: that Day of Account which he considered so improbable that he continuously disputed with me on it has arrived, so now find out where he is and in what state!

"Then he will look and see him in the midst of Hell." Saying this, he himself will look, and see him in the middle of Hell. 'Sawa' means middle... From this, the powers and capabilities of the people of Paradise can be estimated—that sitting on their thrones, they will see whomever they please, no matter how far they might be, and will also speak with them.

"He will say, 'By Allah, you almost ruined me. And if not for the favor of my Lord, I would have been of those brought [into Hell].'" After this, he will rebuke him: 'O wrongdoer, you had almost ruined me as well; it was only the favor of Allah that saved me from your deception. Otherwise, today, just as you are caught in this punishment, I would have been caught as well!'

"'Are we then not to die, Except our first death, and we will not be punished?'" Seeing his end and his own success, he will leap, as it were, with joy, and say to his companions: 'Is it real that we have been saved from the grasp of death forever and are forever carefree from the fear of punishment!' The meaning is: if we have won this game, we have won the greatest game. If you reflect on the style of expression, it emerges that their success will be so far beyond their expectations that even after seeing everything, they will not fully believe their own eyes and will seek confirmation of it from their companions.

"Indeed, this is the great attainment. For the like of this let the workers work." (60-61)

This is a commentary from Allah Almighty on their statement—indicating that the true success is this which will be achieved by those servants of Allah who spend their lives keeping the Hereafter before them; so let whoever wishes to play the game play it for this. The meaning is: those who are active day and night in the love of the world alone, even if they achieve some success, for how many days is it? Ultimately they must die and present themselves before God for reward and punishment one day; so instead of wasting all their efforts for this transient life, why do they not gird up their loins for the eternal success!

The Fate of the People of Hell

After the people of Paradise, this describes the hospitality of the people of Hell. It begins with this question: 'You have heard the description of what the people of Paradise will receive, so now tell me: is that hospitality better or the hospitality of Zaqqum which the people of Hell are to face!'

'Indeed, We have made it a trial...': It states: We have made this tree a trial for the wrongdoers, using which they mocked Hell. This points to the fact that when they were told in the Qur'an that they would have Zaqqum to eat in Hell, instead of taking warning from it, they passed remarks on it—saying: 'Excellent is this Hell—it contains fire, trees, and water!' It states: this tree became a trial for them, and under its cover, they tried to deny a reality which they must necessarily face.

"Indeed, it is a tree..." It states: they wonder how there can be a tree in the fire! The nature of this tree is such that the primary region of its growth is the depths of Hell; it blossoms and bears fruit within it.

An Eloquent Comparison

'Its emerging fruit as if it was the heads of the devils': This comparison is highly eloquent. Sometimes a comparison is made with an imaginary thing, but it is closer to minds than visible and observed things. For example, if you say to a disheveled and untidy person: 'What a devilish appearance you have made!' Although a devil is an imaginary thing, a concept of it is present in everyone's mind; because of this, this comparison will picture that person so beautifully that no other comparison can easily do. Similarly here, the leaves and thorns of the Zaqqum of Hell are compared with the heads of the devils—as if many devils are standing bareheaded. Although this comparison is imaginary, a terrifying concept of the devils is present in minds; because of this, hearing it casts a shudder upon hearts.

"And indeed, they will eat..." It states that this Zaqqum will become their food, and they will be so compelled to eat it that they will fill their stomachs with it, and then to swallow it and digest it, they will drink boiling water over it.

"Then indeed, their return will be to the Hellfire." (68)

This Zaqqum will be given to the people of Hell as a first hospitality (nuzul), as is clarified in verse 62. 'Nuzul' refers to that which is presented before a guest as the initial hospitality. It states that their initial hospitality will be with this Zaqqum and boiling water. Then, after this, their permanent home will be Hell, where the results of all their deeds will appear before them.

"Indeed, they found their fathers astray, So they, in their footsteps, were hastening." (69-70)

The Primary Cause of Deviation

This points to the primary cause of all their deviation—showing that they did not utilize their own intellect and insight, but blindly followed the way of their fathers. Their fathers were astray, and they adopted their very deviation; and when Allah's messengers invited them to utilize their intellect and distinguish between truth and falsehood, they rejected their invitation with great arrogance, saying: 'Are we to leave those deities whom our fathers worshipped at the word of a crazy poet!'... This shows that blind following (taqleed) is among the greatest factors of deviation. In this world, Allah Almighty is testing the intellect and the will of man; for this reason, the one who suspends their intellect and will can never succeed in His test.

"And there had already strayed before them most of the former peoples, And We had already sent among them warners. Then look at how was the end of those who were warned, Except the chosen servants of Allah." (71-74)

The referent of the pronoun in 'before them' (qablahum) is the Quraysh. Now, from here, the discourse turns toward historical proofs. It states: the same kind of deviation in which these (the Quraysh) are caught is that in which the majority of the nations before them were caught and faced the consequences. This points to those nations whose mention has passed in detail in the previous Surahs, and which is also coming below in this Surah. It states that for their reform, We sent Our warners among them to inform them of the end of their wrong way, but like them, in the madness of following their fathers, they rejected the words of these warners; ultimately, they faced that end which necessarily comes upon every nation after warning. Only those servants of Allah remained safe from it whom Allah chose for His mercy... the meaning is: this is the very same thing that is about to come before these people as well, so continue with your work with patience, and leave their matter to Allah.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 75–148

Next, the stories of Hazrat Nuh, Hazrat Ibrahim, Hazrat Ismail, Hazrat Ishaq, Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Harun, Hazrat Ilyas, Hazrat Lut, and Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon them) are described briefly, and at the end of every story is the repetition of the theme: 'Allah blessed His Prophet with mercy and peace, granted success to his invitation, and thus does Allah reward His good servants.' This repetition is, in reality, the core of these stories.

In the mention of the Prophets, the sequence is historical up to Hazrat Harun (peace be upon him); after that, the sequence is thematic. The details of this will follow under the commentary of the verses. Recite the verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And Noah had certainly called Us, and [We are] the best of responders. And We saved him and his family from the great distress.' (75-76)

Hazrat Nuh's Supplication and Allah's Response

This points to that supplication of Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) which he made before his Lord when, after inviting his nation for a long period, he completely despaired of their faith, and which has passed in verses 117-118 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara'. In Surah Nuh, this supplication is quoted in detail, whose final words are:

'And Noah said, "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant. Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except [every] wicked disbeliever."' (26-27)

Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) made this supplication when he had spent his last drop of energy; therefore, Allah Almighty heard his supplication immediately. Consequently, except for those who believed in Hazrat Nuh, all remaining people were drowned. In Surah Nuh, the end of the wicked of his nation is described in these words:

'Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire, and they did not find for themselves besides Allah any helpers.' (25)

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions—indicating: continue facing the situations with patience. Allah Almighty is not unaware of your supplications and efforts. When proof is fully established against the nation, you will see how Allah's help appears, and how your enemies are defeated and destroyed.

"And We saved him and his family from the great distress, And We made his descendants those who remain [on the earth]." (76-77)

It states: We saved Nuh and his companions from that great calamity in which Nuh's entire nation was caught. 'Ahl' refers to all his believing family members, though the word is also used to refer to someone's followers, as we have explained. In the case of Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him), it seems that some people of his progeny believed, and they alone remained those who survived, from whom this world was populated anew. All remaining people were drowned as a retribution for their rebellion.

"And We left for him [favorable mention] among later generations." (78)

The Glad Tidings of Success of the Invitation

In this clause, a prefixed noun (mudaf) and the object of 'tarakna' (We left) are omitted according to the well-known rules of Arabic; if these omissions are spelled out, the complete statement would be: 'We kept a group among later generations upon the way of Nuh.' This points to the success of his invitation—showing that despite all the opposition of his enemies, Allah Almighty granted success to his invitation. His followers were born among the successors, and his enemies were wiped out. In this also lies glad tidings for the Prophet (pbuh)—that Allah Almighty will grant success to your invitation, it will remain firm among subsequent generations, and no one will remain to even take the name of those who are active in opposing you today.

"'Peace upon Noah among the worlds.' Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good. Indeed, he was of Our believing servants. Then We drowned the others." (79-82)

This is praise and appreciation for Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) from Allah Almighty, and the glad tidings of peace and blessing in both this world and the Hereafter—showing that the station he achieved in this world is such as no one achieved before him. He alone won this game among the entire creation.

Eternal Glad Tidings for Good Servants

'Indeed, We thus...': This is a declaration of the reality that this matter is not specific to Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) alone; rather, Allah Almighty has always dealt with His good servants in this way, and in the future too He will deal with His sincere servants in this way. This excellence is not specific to any person, but to attributes. Those who characterize themselves with 'Ihsan' (goodness), Allah Almighty will reward them in this way. The word 'Ihsan' has been explained in various places in this book. Its true reality is the compliance with the commands of Allah Almighty with complete sincerity.

'Indeed, he was of Our believing servants': Here, the word 'mumin' is in its perfect sense. This phrase is also in the sense of praise for Hazrat Nuh from Allah Almighty—meaning: 'Indeed, he was of Our deeply believing servants.' The meaning is: those who have made claims of faith and stood up with its invitation must keep this model before them. This is the true requirement of faith. Those who fulfill the right of faith in this manner achieve the station of 'Ihsan', and for them is this excellence with Allah which Nuh achieved.

'Then We drowned the others': That is, Allah Almighty chose those who possessed capability, and drowned all remaining people. This is the very objective of the creation of this world. From within it, Allah Almighty will choose those who prove their capability. All remaining people He will cast into Hell like dry wood.

"And indeed, among his kind was Abraham." (83)

Hazrat Ibrahim and His Invitation

After Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him), the most glorious prophet and messenger is Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Regarding him, He states: 'Indeed, he is from his kind and group.' The invitation he presented to the creation was the very same invitation which Hazrat Nuh presented, and the faith and sincerity he showed in the way of Allah was the very same sincerity which Hazrat Nuh showed. In verse 78 above, the statement: 'We kept a group among later generations upon the way of Nuh' points to this very continuity—showing that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) arose as the inviter to that very way of which Hazrat Nuh was the inviter. It should be clear here that the way (Millah) of all prophets is, in reality, one, and that is Islam! The detailed discussion on this has passed in its proper place.

Here, it is also noteworthy that from traditions, it appears that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was trained by Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him). Although I am not fully satisfied with this tradition, if its correctness is accepted, another aspect of Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) being of Hazrat Nuh's (peace be upon him) group also emerges.

This is an expression of Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) perfect uprightness, his complete devotion to Allah, and his perfect sincerity. 'Qalb saleem' (sound heart) refers to that heart which is entirely safe and pure from the diseases of polytheism and hypocrisy. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara', it is: 'The Day when there will not benefit [anyone] wealth or children, But only one who comes to Allah with a sound heart.' (88-89)—meaning: neither will their hearts have any disease of polytheism and hypocrisy, nor will their loyalty be divided; rather, their hearts will be pure from every impurity, and all their love and obedience will be for the One Allah alone.

"[Remember] when he said to his father and his people, 'What is that which you worship? Is it falsehood - gods other than Allah - that you desire?'" (85-86)

To describe Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) uprightness and his perfect sincerity and Tawhid, this refers to that call which he presented to his father and his people.

'What is that which you worship?'—these words are for expressing contempt. That is, he reproached them: 'What are these insignificant things that you are worshipping!'

'Ifk' means lie and baseless thing. That is: 'Do you desire other deities besides God merely as a lie, fabricated by yourselves!'

"'Then what is your thought about the Lord of the worlds?'" (87)

This sentence is as brief in words as it is vast in meaning. The meaning is: if you desire other deities besides God, then what is your thought about the Lord of the worlds? Are you in the misconception that He alone is incapable of managing your needs and protecting you? Are you in the illusion that He alone is unable to manage this world, and therefore you have sought helpers for Him? Are you in the doubt that He cannot be aware of every corner of this vast world and of the states of every individual, and therefore other means and mediums are required to inform Him? Despairing of His justice and mercy, have you set up intercessors in His court according to your own assumption to achieve His mercy? The meaning is: unless a person possesses some such misconception about the Generous Lord, they would never tolerate making anyone besides Him their deity! And if a person harbors any such misconception about their Lord, they must remember that—God forbid—God is not a being devoid of honor and jealousy that He would tolerate any partnership and interference in His essence and His kingdom; rather, He is Severe, Powerful, Jealous, and Grand; therefore, He will cast all those into Hell who claim to be partners in His divinity or make others partners.

"And he cast a look at the stars And said, 'Indeed, I am ill.' So they turned away from him, departing. Then he turned to their gods and said, 'Do you not eat? What is [wrong] with you that you do not speak?' Then he turned upon them striking with [his] right hand." (88-93)

Clarifying the Insignificance of Their Deities before the People

The aforementioned speech was delivered by Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), it seems, in the temple or on the occasion of a festival. After this, he made a plan to clarify the insignificance of their deities before the people of his nation. For this, the method he adopted was that when the people began to depart from the temple in the night after finishing the festival, he cast a look at the stars as if he wanted to estimate the time, and saying words to be heard by the people, said: 'I am feeling exhausted.' The people understood that the night had passed and this young man was exhausted from the day's work; therefore, he did not want to go home at that time, but wanted to spend the night in the temple. This was an ordinary thing; because of this, the people left him there and departed to their homes. When Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) found himself alone, avoiding all eyes, he entered the temple, passed some sarcastic remarks on the idols—asking why they did not eat the food placed before them, and why there was such silence, and why they did not speak! After this, he struck some powerful blows that broke them into pieces. In another place, it is mentioned that he broke all the small idols, leaving only the large idol for a specific wisdom which has been explained in Surah Al-Anbiya.

Correcting a Misconception of the Commentators

This is the simple and actual form of the event that emerges from the words of the Qur'an, but commentators have—who knows on what basis—quoted such traditions under this which suggest that—God forbid—Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) spoke a lie on this occasion and believed in the influence of the stars.

 Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) cast a look at the stars not because he believed in their influence like the star-worshippers, but merely because he wanted to estimate the time. Before the custom of clocks, when people had to estimate the time in the night, they looked at the stars, and during the day, at the sun; just as in this era, to find out the time, every person's eye goes to their wristwatch, in nomadic life, for this purpose, every person's eye spontaneously rises to the sky. Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) also cast a look at the stars for this very purpose. It had no relation to the superstitions of the star-worshippers or to the influence of the stars.

Similarly, his stating: 'Indeed, I am ill' (Inni saqeem) was also not a lie on his part. 'Saqeem' in the Arabic language is used for sick, exhausted, fatigued, weak, and frail. A speech which is not robust is also called 'saqeem'. Below in this Surah, in the mention of Hazrat Yunus, it is: 'So We cast him onto the open shore while he was ill' (145); obviously, this word is used here in the sense of weak and frail. Above, we pointed out that this was the occasion of a festival in the temple. On such occasions, every person becomes exhausted to some extent. Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) also felt this quality, and since he intended to spend the night in the temple, by casting a look at the sky and saying this phrase, he gave the managers and guardians of the temple the impression that due to exhaustion and the passing of the night, he did not want to go home at that time, but wanted to stay there. In this way, although he made a subtle implication (tawriyah) for staying in the temple, which misled the guardians of the temple, the method Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) adopted and the words he uttered contained neither any lie nor any bad belief; rather, this was a pure subtle implication of the kind of which some excellent examples have passed before.

'So they turned away from him, departing': This describes the reaction of the guardians of the temple. From Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) attitude and his phrase, they assumed that this young man was feeling exhausted and, since the night had passed, did not want to go home. Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) intention appeared entirely harmless to them; therefore, they did not think it fit to interfere, but left him and went to their places.

'Then he turned to their gods...': 'Rawghan' (turning to) in Arabic is used to go somewhere secretly, avoiding eyes. This shows that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) reached the idols at a time when he estimated that the guardians had slept. At that time, he rose silently, and avoiding all eyes, reached that part of the temple where the idols were kept.

'and said, "Do you not eat? What is [wrong] with you that you do not speak?"': First he passed some sarcastic remarks on them—asking why they did not eat the delicious food and sweets placed before them by their worshippers, and why they were so silent, and why they did not speak! These sarcastic remarks show that when Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) took this action, there was no sign of panic upon him; rather, he was in a highly pleasant mood that according to his plan, he had got an excellent opportunity to entertain these deities, and his plan was entirely successful.

'Then he turned upon them striking with [his] right hand': The original construction of the sentence is: 'Fara'gha 'alayhim bi-darbihim darban bil-yameen'—therefore, its correct meaning is: 'Then he fell upon them, striking them powerful blows.' 'Yameen' refers to the right hand, and since the blow of the right hand is powerful, here it is used in the sense of striking powerful blows.

The Guardians' Attitude toward Hazrat Ibrahim

Here, some part of the story is omitted, which is clarified in Surah Al-Anbiya. When Hazrat Ibrahim broke the idols, no one knew. Later, when the guardians came to know of this accident and the question arose: whose doing could this be? Since Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had been criticizing the idols and had also threatened that he was going to make a plan against them, the managers of the temple decided that this must be his doing; so all of them ran to him and began to question him. First he mocked them, and pointing to the large idol, said: 'This must have broken them,' and to draw attention to their foolishness, added: 'Why do you ask me, why do you not ask your deities upon whom this calamity has fallen! If they can neither save themselves from a calamity nor even speak of it, then for what are they useful that you are worshipping them!' At this confrontation of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), first they were highly ashamed, but then ancestral pride overcame them and they said: 'How can we ask them, you already know that they do not speak?' After this admission of theirs, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) reproached them thoroughly, being enraged by which they conspired to burn him in the fire, but Allah Almighty made their conspiracy fail.

"He said, 'Do you worship that which you [yourselves] carve, While Allah created you and that which you do?'" (95-96)

Reproaching the Nation after the Final Establishment of Proof

Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) reproached his nation in this manner when he had fully established proof against them, as we have pointed out. He said: 'O unfortunate ones! You worship the idols of wood and stone carved by your own hands! Allah is worshipped because He created us, but your intellect is so lost that you worship those whom you carve with your own hands! As if you yourselves are the creators of your creators! Remember that Allah alone is He Who created you and also created these woods and stones from which you carve your deities, and also created those Jinn and angels whose forms you carve.'... Some theologians have derived from 'and that which you do' (wama ta'maloon) the conclusion that Allah Almighty is also the Creator of human actions. This derivation from this verse is, in our view, entirely out of place. We have explained its correct interpretation, so there remains no need to refute it.

"They said, 'Build for him a structure and throw him into the burning fire.' So they intended for him a plot, but We made them the lowest." (97-98)

The Conspiracy to Throw Hazrat Ibrahim into the Fire and Allah's Protection

After this open opposition of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), the managers of the temple decided to burn him in the fire, but they wanted to do this through a secret plot instead of doing it openly. This is clear from 'So they intended for him a plot' (fa'aradoo bihi kayda). The reason for adopting the form of a plot (kayd) can also be that Hazrat Ibrahim does not escape from their hands, and it is also possible that in case of an open action, they feared some resistance from Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) family. In the nomadic period of life, family bias was a highly important thing. Thus, for a long time, the Quraysh hesitated to take any open action against the Prophet (pbuh) due to this very fear.

The question of what plan they adopted to throw Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) into the fire is a difficult question. Nothing is found in the Qur'an or the Torah in the light of which a decisive answer can be given. From the verses under discussion, it is learned only that the priests of the temple made a plan to build a fireplace and carry Hazrat Ibrahim to it under some excuse and then throw him into the fire. From another place in the Qur'an, it is learned that he was thrown into the fire, but Allah Almighty protected him from the harm of the fire by His power and mercy, and the enemies' plot failed.

"And he said, 'Indeed, I am going to my Lord; He will guide me.'" (99)

Hazrat Ibrahim's Courage

After this event, despairing of the faith of his nation, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) resolved to migrate, relying on Allah Almighty. The decision to migrate is a highly important decision. A prophet resolves to cut himself off from his environment and enter an entirely new environment, and there is no estimate of whether this new environment will prove compatible for him or incompatible; for this reason, in this path, his entire reliance is on the support and guidance of Allah Almighty. In 'He will guide me' (sayahdeen), this very reliance is expressed—showing that although the conditions are entirely hidden, there is hope from Allah Almighty that He will guide. His promise is that those who strive in His path, He will open paths for them.

"'My Lord, grant me [a child] from among the righteous.'" (100)

The Desire for Companions

After cutting oneself off from one's relatives, family, and tribe, the thing whose need is most felt is good companions; thus, along with migration, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) supplicated: 'O my Lord, grant me good companions in place of these bad people.' In my view, this supplication of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was not only for righteous progeny, but also for good companions and helpers, in whom righteous progeny is included first and foremost.

"So We gave him good tidings of a forbearing boy." (101)

The Acceptance of the Supplication and the Glad Tidings of Hazrat Ismail's Birth

Allah Almighty accepted this supplication and gave him the glad tidings of the birth of a boy; by this boy, obviously, Hazrat Ismail is meant. Some of its reasons are entirely obvious:

1. The primary reason is that he was the only child of Hazrat Ibrahim at that time, for whom he had supplicated. Regarding Hazrat Ishaq, we have explained in its proper place that Hazrat Ibrahim did not supplicate for him, but he was bestowed upon him as a reward for Hazrat Ismail's sacrifice.

2. The second reason is that below, his sacrifice is mentioned, and Hazrat Ibrahim sacrificed Hazrat Ismail, not Hazrat Ishaq. The distortions which the Jews have made in this event have been completely exposed by the mentor-scholar (ra) in his treatise *Dhabih*. Those who seek details should study it.

3. The third reason is that the attribute of this boy is mentioned here as 'forbearing' (haleem). This attribute is an expression of his resolve and steadfastness which he showed under the knife of his father, and in reward for which Allah Almighty blessed him with the titles of 'truthful to his promise', 'patient', and 'forbearing'. It should be kept in mind here that this very same attribute 'haleem' has occurred in the Qur'an for Hazrat Ibrahim as well. This shows that Hazrat Ismail was the most prominent manifestation of his father's attributes.

"And when he reached with him the age of exertion, he said, 'O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think.' He said, 'O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast.'" (102)

The Event of Hazrat Ismail's Sacrifice

'Exertion' (Al-Sa'y) refers to the age of running and moving about. The meaning is: when this dear boy reached the age of running and moving about with his father, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) saw in a dream that he was being instructed by Allah Almighty to sacrifice him for the sake of his Lord. Above, it has been pointed out that Hazrat Ismail was received by Hazrat Ibrahim as a result of his supplication when he had left his family and his nation and migrated to another country, and the only thing which could be a source of comfort for him in this exile was this only boy. The age of this boy at that time was such that he walked and moved about with his father and assisted him in his small works. From the Torah, it is learned that his age at that time was 13 years. Everyone can estimate that at this age, a son is most beloved in the eyes of his father. Reflect on how severe a test it is in which Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is placed. But no one can be perfect in loyalty to Allah Almighty unless they are ready to sacrifice their most beloved thing in His way; for this reason, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was placed in this test. Although this instruction was given in a dream, and a dream is subject to interpretation, had Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) wished, he could have made some interpretation of it; but since he was a truthful and loyal servant, instead of seeking any interpretation, he prepared to comply with it literally.

'he said, "O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think"': The first step he took in this path was to estimate the resolve of his son as well. He said: 'O my son, indeed I continuously see in a dream that I am sacrificing you, so consider what is your opinion.' The style of the discourse suggests that this dream appeared to Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) more than once. If it had appeared only once, the style 'Indeed, I saw in a dream' (Inni ra'aytu fi al-manami) would have been more suitable. Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) also clarified that although this dream appeared to him more than once, it is after all a dream, so that the actual nature of the matter became entirely clear to his son. This shows that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had trust in the intellect and understanding of his son even at that time. He expected that his opinion in this matter would assist him in making the correct decision.

'He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast"': Hazrat Ismail accepted this dream as the command of Allah instead of a mere dream, and immediately replied: 'O my father, comply with the command you are being given without hesitation, and be assured regarding me. If Allah wills, you will find me completely steadfast.'

"And when they had both submitted and he put him down upon his forehead," (103)

The Actual Stage

Now, this describes the actual stage, i.e., the sacrifice. 'Aslama' (they submitted) means they both handed over themselves to Allah. That is, both father and son prepared for the test. The father drew the knife to sacrifice his son, and the son handed over himself to be sacrificed. 'And he put him down upon his forehead'—and the father laid his son down upon his forehead. Some have explained laying him down upon his forehead by stating that Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) wanted that the beloved face of his son should not be before him at the time of sacrifice so that affection of the heart does not prevent the drawing of the knife; but this explanation is, in my view, incorrect. The father who had prepared to draw the knife upon his only and beloved son in this manner would not stand in need of such consolations. In my view, the reason is that he wanted to sacrifice his son in the state of prostration; therefore, he laid him down upon his forehead. The posture of prostration is the most beloved posture of proximity to God in Islam as well. As is stated: 'and prostrate and draw near' (Al-Alaq: 19), and this status has been accepted in ancient religions as well. It should also be kept in mind that this event occurred at a time when the House of Allah was neither constructed nor any specified Qiblah was appointed for worship. If any specified Qiblah had been present, it is possible that Hazrat Ibrahim also laid his son facing the Qiblah, just as we lay animals facing the Qiblah at the time of sacrifice.

"We called to him, 'O Abraham, You have fulfilled the vision.' Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good." (104-105)

Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) Victory in the Test

It was close that Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) knife would draw, but Allah Almighty called him: 'O Abraham! You have made the dream true, and this victory which you achieved in this test is the reward of your goodness, sincerity, and Ihsan. Allah Almighty thus rewards His good servants in His tests.'

'You have fulfilled the vision': We have pointed out above that what is shown in a dream is subject to interpretation. The actual interpretation of this dream shown to Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was that he should dedicate this son to God. It was not intended that he should actually sacrifice him. Thus, when he prepared to sacrifice his son, the actual objective of the dream was fulfilled; because of this, Allah Almighty gave him the glad tidings that the purpose of the dream was fulfilled and no further action was required. Those who have derived from this the conclusion that Allah Almighty abrogated His command before its execution have resorted to an unnecessary affectation. Once the correct interpretation is clarified, there remains no need to refute it.

'Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good' clarifies the reality that those who adopt the path of Ihsan (goodness) regarding sincerity and compliance with the commands of Allah—meaning their efforts are to comply with His every command in the manner that it deserves to be complied with—Allah Almighty rewards them by granting them magnificent successes in His great tests, and then in reward for them they achieve the victories of the eternal kingdom of the Hereafter. On the contrary, those whose approach regarding religion remains escapist, they gradually lose the capability to face the trials of God, the result of which is that ultimately the path of success in the Hereafter is closed upon them.

"Indeed, this was the clear trial." (106)

Allah's Praise for Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) Success

This is praise and appreciation for Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) from Allah Almighty upon this success—stating that this was no ordinary test, but a highly great test in which Abraham (peace be upon him) won! The test which Allah Himself declares a great test, what doubt can there be in its being great? And if Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) achieved such a success in it, the appreciation of which was given by Allah Himself with the magnificent words: 'You have made the dream true,' then there is no doubt that under this sky, neither did any greater test occur, nor did anyone achieve a more magnificent success.

"And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice," (107)

The Permanent Memorial of This Sacrifice

It states: We ransomed Ismail with a great sacrifice. This points to the fact that We instructed Ibrahim (peace be upon him) to sacrifice a ram in place of this son, and this sacrifice will remain forever among the subsequent generations as a memorial of this event in the form of a great sacrifice. This is that very sacrifice which is included in the rituals of Hajj, and has been continuously performed from the time of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) until today, and will remain until the Resurrection... It should be kept in mind here that although sacrifice has been continuously performed in all religions from the time of Hazrat Adam, no sacrifice in the world has achieved this greatness, importance, and vastness which this sacrifice of Hazrat Ibrahim achieved.

"And We left for him [favorable mention] among later generations." (108)

This is that very same repetition which passed above in the story of Hazrat Nuh. It has been explained that among the successors, Allah Almighty kept a group upon the way of Ibrahim. In verse 84 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara', this supplication of Hazrat Ibrahim has passed: 'And grant me a reputation of honor among later generations.' Allah Almighty accepted his supplication and blessed him with that honor of eternal fame, the example of which cannot be found.

"Peace upon Abraham." (109)

This is an independent sentence—meaning: upon Abraham (peace be upon him) is peace and blessing in this world and the Hereafter, as a reward for his sincerity. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Salam' (peace) is to magnify its status.

"Thus do We reward the doers of good." (110)

'Thus' points to this very peace and blessing. It states: We thus bless Our good servants with Our mercies and blessings.

"Indeed, he was of Our believing servants." (111)

The word 'mumin' is used here in its true meaning. It states: indeed Abraham (peace be upon him) was among Our true believing servants. This shows that the true faith is that which contains that spirit of sincerity and Ihsan of which Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) presented the model. The faith of every claimant is not credible with Allah.

"And We gave him good tidings of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous." (112)

The Glad Tidings of Hazrat Ishaq's Birth

As a reward for success in this test, Allah Almighty gave Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) the glad tidings of the birth of his second son, Hazrat Ishaq, and along with this glad tidings, also informed him of the fact that he would be among the righteous and Allah Almighty would bless him with the office of prophethood. The mention of these attributes along with the glad tidings was necessary because for Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), the mere birth of a son was not a matter of great importance. Having received Hazrat Ismail, he was entirely satisfied regarding progeny; thus, from the Torah, it is learned that when he was given the glad tidings of Hazrat Ishaq, he said: 'I wish Ismail might live before You!' However, the thing which could be glad tidings for him was that the son to be born would be righteous and a prophet.

From this, it became entirely clear that the traditions which the Jews have fabricated regarding Hazrat Ishaq's being the one sacrificed are entirely baseless. Hazrat Ishaq was born after the event of the sacrifice. He is not the son who was sacrificed, but the reward of the sacrifice which Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) received in this world.

"And We blessed him and Isaac. And among their descendants is the doer of good and the clearly unjust to himself." (113)

The Referent of the Pronoun

The referent of the pronoun in 'upon him' ('alayhi) is Hazrat Ismail. The view of those who have considered Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) as the referent is entirely incorrect. If the referent had been Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), then after this, 'from his descendants' (min dhurriyyatihi) would have been suitable instead of 'from their descendants' (min dhurriyyatihima); because Hazrat Ishaq is not a rival of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), but is from his very essence.

A Reminder to the Descendants of Hazrat Ismail and Hazrat Ishaq

It states: We blessed both Ismail and Ishaq in this world. The progeny of both flourished. Among their descendants, some are good, sincere believers walking in the footsteps of their righteous fathers, and some have completely deviated from their way to fall into disbelief and polytheism, and have thus become clearly unjust to their own souls. This is a timely reminder to the progeny of both—the Children of Ismail and the Children of Israel—so that they are warned whose pure descendants they are, what way they were supposed to adopt, and where they are wandering.

Answering a Grammatical Question

[In the analysis of the story of the sacrifice], it began with 'And when' (Falamma), and reached up to here, and it remained unknown what is the apodosis (jawab) of 'lamma'? We have mentioned in more than one place in this book that sometimes in 'lamma', 'idha', and other such conditional sentences, the apodosis is omitted, and the force of the speaker's discourse stands in place of that omitted clause. This method is adopted particularly in those places where the nature of the apodosis is such that words are incapable of expressing it. An obvious example of this will also come in Surah Az-Zumar: 'And those who feared their Lord will be driven to Paradise in groups until, when they reach it while its gates have been opened and its keepers say to them, "Peace be upon you; you have become pure, so enter it to abide eternally," [they will enter].' (73). See, in this verse as well, the apodosis of 'idha' is omitted, which is obvious from the force of the discourse itself. Similarly, in the verse under discussion, the apodosis is omitted, words are incapable of expressing it, but it is obvious. The meaning is: when both father and son took this step for the pleasure of their Lord, imagine what magnificent mercies of Allah Almighty they must have deserved!

"And We certainly conferred favor upon Moses and Aaron. And We saved them and their people from the great distress, And We supported them so it was they who overcame." (114-116)

Hazrat Musa and His Nation

After Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), the most glorious messengers are Hazrat Musa and Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them), who defeated a tyrant enemy to become the founders of a magnificent nation, and whose Shariah remained dominant over a vast world for a long period. It states: We also blessed Musa and Harun with the favor of Our Shariah and guidance, and saved them and their people—the Children of Israel—from a highly great distress, i.e., the slavery of the Egyptians, and helped them, so ultimately they were the ones who overcame. 'We supported them' also points to those victories which the Children of Israel achieved as a nation against their enemies after leaving Egypt.

"And We gave them the explicit Scripture. And We guided them to the straight path." (117-118)

The Beginning of Divine Teaching in the Form of a Book Was with the Torah

'Al-Kitab al-Mustabeen' (explicit Scripture) and 'Al-Kitab al-Mubeen' (clear Book) share the same meaning. This refers to the Torah. Before this, Allah Almighty did not bestow His Shariah upon any nation in the form of a clear book, but people were given only oral teaching. The Torah holds this special distinction—that in it, the Shariah was given in the form of a structured and clear book.

Although the Torah was revealed to Hazrat Musa, since Hazrat Harun was made Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) partner in messengership by Allah Almighty, He associated him in this honor of its bestowal as well, and regarding both, stated: We guided them to the straight path. Within this clause, the allegations which the Jews have raised against Hazrat Harun (peace be upon him) are refuted, the refutation of which has passed in detail in their proper places.

"'And We left for them [favorable mention] among later generations: Peace upon Moses and Aaron.' Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good. Indeed, they were of Our believing servants." (119-122)

This is that very same repetition which has passed above, and every part of which has been explained. The meaning is: the way of these two messengers also remained after them. Upon them was peace and blessing from Allah, and their enemies were disgraced. Allah Almighty thus rewards His good and loyal servants. The meaning is: today also, those who show this sincerity for God and His religion will deserve God's mercy and blessing in this way, Allah Almighty will grant permanence to their work, the followers of their invitation will be born, and their name will remain respected in history.

Mention of Some Prophets Briefly: Hazrat Ilyas

Up to here, the mention was of the glorious prophets of history, and the sequence in their mention is historical. Next, Hazrat Ilyas, Hazrat Lut, and Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon them) are pointed to briefly.

By Ilyas, the same is meant who is mentioned in the Torah by the name of Elijah, and whose time is stated to be 789 BC. In verse 85 of Surah Al-An'am, his name has occurred along with Hazrat Zakariyya, Hazrat Yahya, and Hazrat Isa (peace be upon them), which shows that in terms of attributes, he belongs to the category of these prophets. Some have interpreted him as Hazrat Idris, but this view is incorrect. The mention of Hazrat Idris is present in the Qur'an by his name. Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him) was Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) relative, his contemporary, and was trained by him. Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him) has been mentioned in detail in Surah Yunus and Surah Al-Anbiya—showing that overcome by the feeling of divine jealousy, he migrated before the command of Allah Almighty, upon which he was rebuked, and then returned to his nation, and through his invitation, his entire nation believed.

The Objective of Mentioning These Prophets

The objective of mentioning these prophets is also to clarify that very reality which was clarified in the mention of the glorious prophets above—showing that although these prophets did not achieve that strength and followers which Nuh, Ibrahim, and Musa achieved, and they had to work in an environment entirely alien to them, yet Allah Almighty granted success to their work as well, and after them, their invitation and their way remained. The mention of Hazrat Yunus at the very end holds this importance: in the work of inviting to the truth, haste—even if motivated by jealousy for the truth—is not permissible; rather, the prophet must continue with his work with patience and steadfastness. Only Allah knows how long the spark of life remains in a nation, and when its cup is filled.

"And indeed, Elias was of the messengers, When he said to his people, 'Will you not fear Allah? Do you call upon Ba'l and leave the best of creators - Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers?'" (123-126)

Hazrat Ilyas's Invitation

Ba'l is the name of that idol which Hazrat Ilyas's nation worshipped; the context is proof that the city of Baalbek was named after this idol, because 'bek' is the abbreviation of 'Bakkah' which means city. He invited his nation: 'O unfortunate ones! Leaving the best of creators, Allah, Who is your Lord and the Lord of all your ancestors, do you worship Ba'l!' 'the best of creators' (Ahsan-al-Khaliqeen) is an expression of the reality that He is not only the Creator, but the best of creators. He created, He bestowed all apparent and hidden capabilities, He is nurturing, and He alone is your Lord and the Lord of all your ancestors; so what sense is there in leaving Him to worship Ba'l? What share does he have in your creation and lordship?

"And they denied him, so indeed they will be brought [into Hell], Except the chosen servants of Allah." (127-128)

It states that his nation denied him, as a retribution for which all deniers will be brought before Allah Almighty as criminals. Only those will remain safe from it whom Allah chose for His worship and who believed in Hazrat Ilyas.

"And We left for him [favorable mention] among later generations:" (129)
