This is that very same repetition which has passed in the previous stories—meaning those who opposed Ilyas left no name, but Allah kept the name and the work of Ilyas.

"'Peace upon Ilyaseen.' Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good. Indeed, he was of Our believing servants." (130-132)

'Ilyaseen' is, in my view, the plural of Ilyas, and refers to all his family and followers. In Arabic, when the plural of a name is brought in this manner, it refers to all its parts and branches. In the Qur'an, 'Toore Seneen' is used for the plural of Toor based on this principle. This clarifies the reality that the peace and blessing sent upon a prophet also includes all his loyal companions.

'Indeed, We thus...'—its explanation has passed above. The objective of its repetition is that this is a Sunnah of Allah. Those who show faith and Ihsan, Allah Almighty keeps their name alive, and those who oppose them leave no name.

"And indeed, Lot was of the messengers, [Remember] when We saved him and his family, all, Except an old woman [who remained] among those who remained behind." (133-135)

Hazrat Lut's Call

This refers to the story of Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him) and his nation. It states that he was also among Our messengers. His nation also denied him, which resulted in Our saving him along with all his believing family members from Our punishment, and destroying all remaining people. Among his family, an old woman—Hazrat Lut's wife—due to being deprived of faith, became among those who remained behind; thus, she was also destroyed along with those who were destroyed. This is because a mere relationship with a prophet is of no use to anyone; the useful thing is faith and righteous deeds.

A Timely Warning to the Quraysh

At the end, instead of that repetition which has passed with all the previous stories, here He addresses the Quraysh—stating that you pass by their settlements in your commercial journeys continuously, by day and by night; so why do you not think that the God Who always dealt in this way with the deniers of His messengers, why would His Sunnah change in your case! It is as if the repetition was automatic here; because of this, in its place, a timely warning was given to the addressees, for which the location of Lut's nation provided an excellent opportunity.

"And indeed, Jonah was of the messengers, [Remember] when he fled to the laden ship." (139-140)

Hazrat Yunus's Story

Hazrat Yunus's (peace be upon him) story has passed in Surah Yunus and Surah Al-Anbiya, etc. The confusion which the narrators of the Torah have made in this story has been discussed in detail in its proper place. He was sent as a messenger to the people of Nineveh. His nation disregarded his invitation, so, overcome by jealousy for the truth, he left the nation and boarded a ship which was entirely ready to sail. On the way, the ship was surrounded by a storm. The sailors, according to their custom, decided that some criminal had boarded the ship, and until he was separated and thrown into the sea, the ship could not escape from this destruction. Ultimately, to specify the criminal, lots had to be cast; the lot fell to Hazrat Yunus, and he was thrown into the sea. In the sea, a shark swallowed him, but Allah Almighty saved him because of his exalting Him, and the fish threw him onto the sand of the shore. There, to protect him from the intense heat of the sun on the shore, Allah Almighty grew a gourd plant, whose shade protected him in that state of weakness. Ultimately, when he regained his strength, Allah Almighty instructed him to go to his nation again and invite them. This time, he invited them, and the entire population of Nineveh, whose number was around one hundred or one hundred and fifty thousand, believed.

This story is narrated to the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions so that they also do not lose heart at the nation's attitude, but continue with the work of invitation. A prophet is appointed by Allah Almighty on a front of the conflict of truth and falsehood; for this reason, it is not permissible for him to leave this front without God's command, even if his motivation is some good feeling. The trials which Allah Almighty has destined for His messenger, he must pass through them; if he hesitates in facing a trial, it is possible that in trying to escape from one trial, he gets caught in another greater trial. Similarly, the respite which the nation must receive for the establishment of proof is also essential according to the divine law, and God alone knows better when proof was established against a nation. Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him) decided according to his own estimation that the people of Nineveh were not going to believe, whereas facts proved that the entire nation possessed the capability to believe.

The Nature of Hazrat Yunus's Lapse

'when he fled to the laden ship'—'Abaqa' (fled) points to the event when Hazrat Yunus left the nation to go to some other place. The honorable Prophets never stumble in following the self, but in the zeal of following the truth, they also sometimes exceed the limits. Although this is not bad in itself, but rather desirable, since the honorable Prophets are the touchstone of truth, if any excess occurs on their part even in this aspect, Allah Almighty takes them to account. Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him) committed a similar lapse. When his nation disregarded his invitation, he departed from there in sadness—thinking what was the use of throwing pearls before such ungrateful ones. It seems that when he resolved to go somewhere, immediately he found a laden ship ready to sail. 'Mashhoon' means a laden and filled ship. By bringing this word, it is intended to point to the reality that he wanted to leave, and according to his desire, he found a ship ready, and boarded it without hesitation. He did not get an opportunity to reflect on all aspects of the issue.

"And he drew lots and was among the losers." (141)

It seems that according to the tradition to which we have pointed in the preface, when the turn came to cast lots, the sailors chose Hazrat Yunus to perform this service. The reason must be that he appeared to be the most honest and dignified person in the ship, and casting lots was suitable only for an honest man. But see the change of nature: the lot fell to Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him) himself, and according to this decision of the unseen, he was thrown into the sea. It should be kept in mind here that 'Abada' (fled) is used for a runaway slave who has left his master's duty; as if Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him) was declared a runaway slave and was thrown into the sea by the sailors as a retribution for this crime.

"Then the fish swallowed him, while he was blameworthy." (142)

'Hoot' (fish) refers to some large kind of sea fish. In the sea, there are fish of the kind of whales and sharks which can swallow a human whole. Some such fish swallowed Hazrat Yunus. 'while he was blameworthy' (wa huwa muleem)—meaning this calamity which befell him was because he deserved it, as he had committed an action upon which Allah Almighty held him deserving of warning. We have pointed out above that although Hazrat Yunus took this step out of a good feeling, he took it without Allah's permission; for this reason, he was taken to account, and since accounting is in accordance with a person's rank and station, the accounting was severe.

"And if he had not been of those who exalt Allah, He would have remained inside its belly until the Day they are resurrected." (143-144)

Regarding the exalting (Tasbeeh) pointed out here, it has been clarified in another place that this refers to his reciting: 'There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers.' This is an excellent word of admitting one's mistake and seeking forgiveness of Allah Almighty which flowed from Hazrat Yunus's (peace be upon him) tongue, and this word saved him from this grasp. It seems that as soon as this calamity befell Hazrat Yunus (peace be upon him), he was warned that this was a rebuke from Allah Almighty, and he turned to his Lord with all his heart. Allah Almighty has described the glory of this word: this alone saved him; otherwise, the belly of that fish which swallowed him would have become his grave until the Day of Resurrection.

"But We cast him onto the open shore while he was ill. And We caused to grow over him a gourd vine." (145-146)

By the command of Allah Almighty, ultimately the fish threw him onto a sand bank. Obviously, at that time, he must have been entirely exhausted and weak. But Allah Almighty arranged for a gourd-like vine for him there, whose shade protected him from the intense heat of the sun on the shore, and after some time, he regained his strength.

"And We sent him to [a population of] a hundred thousand or more. And they believed, so We gave them enjoyment for a time." (147-148)

When he regained his strength, Allah Almighty sent him again to his nation to invite them, whose number was one hundred thousand or even more, and all these people believed, and Allah Almighty saved them from the punishment and gave them the opportunity to enjoy life until their appointed time. 'Aw' (or) also comes in the sense of 'bal' (rather). From the books of the Torah, it is learned that the number of those who believed was one hundred and fifty thousand.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 149–182

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah. The Surah ends on the same theme with which it began, and we have pointed out in various Surahs this characteristic of the style of the Qur'an—that the theme with which the Surah began is also that on which it ends.

Have a look at the initial verses: this Surah began with the refutation of the divinity of the angels and the knowledge of the unseen of the Jinn. After this, to warn the Quraysh, the stories of the Prophets were described, clarifying the reality that all prophets invited to the very same thing to which they are being invited today, and also clarifying the reality that those who denied the messengers faced destruction and Allah Almighty sent His blessing and mercy upon His messengers and their companions. In the world, their name and work achieved honor and permanence, and in the Hereafter is the glad tidings of the eternal kingdom. Exactly this same theme has appeared at the end in a new style. First, the polytheists of the Quraysh are challenged: if you have any intellectual or traditional proof for worshipping the angels, believing them to be the daughters of God, and for the relationship you have joined between the Jinn and God, then present it; otherwise, fear the wrath of God. Then, the actual position of the angels before God is clarified through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel, so that those who have believed the angels to be the daughters of God and are worshipping them are struck by this statement from the greatest of the angels—stating what rank and station the angels describe for themselves and what their foolish worshippers assume. Finally, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled—stating that the promise of victory which has been made to Allah's messengers will also be fulfilled for you; therefore, continue with your work with patience, and your opponents will soon see their end... Recite the verses in this light.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'Then inquire of them, "Does your Lord have daughters while they have sons?"' (149)

The Connection of This Discourse with the Initial Theme

This discourse is conjoined, as we have pointed out, with the initial theme of the Surah. Although in terms of words there appears to be a distance between the two parts of the discourse, this distance is only apparent; in terms of reality, the discourse is entirely reconnected. After refuting the divinity of the angels and the Jinn at the beginning, in verse 11, with this very word 'Fastaftihim' (Then inquire of them), the discourse turned toward warning the Quraysh and consoling the Prophet (pbuh), and that theme continued in full sequence up to here. After its conclusion, conjoining on this 'Fastaftihim', the primary issue is taken up again—stating: ask those who are worshipping the angels, believing them to be the daughters of God, that they choose sons for themselves, so why have they chosen daughters for their Lord? The meaning is: first of all, attributing sons and daughters to God is in itself a severe kind of ignorance, but they have added to this ignorance by choosing for God what they dislike for themselves. Whereas reason and nature require that attributes suitable to His majesty are attributed to God, they have not only committed this injustice of attributing to Him an attribute contrary to His majesty, but have attributed an attribute which they themselves consider a disgrace.

"Or did We create the angels as females while they were witnesses?" (150)

That is, with what confidence are they making this claim that the angels are the daughters of God—on what proof are they saying this? Were they present when We created the angels! In Surah Az-Zukhruf, this is expressed in these words: 'And they have made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females. Did they witness their creation? Their testimony will be recorded, and they will be questioned.' (19).

"Unquestionably, it is of their falsehood that they say, 'Allah has begotten,' and indeed, they are liars." (151-152)

That is, they possess neither any intellectual nor any traditional proof, nor did they witness it; they are merely claiming according to their own assumption that God has progeny. It states that these people are entirely liars.

"Has He chosen daughters over sons? What is [wrong] with you? How do you make judgement? Then will you not be reminded?" (153-155)

That is, if He had desired progeny, why did He choose daughters over sons? When He could create sons as well, why did He tolerate for Himself what you tolerate for yourselves only under compulsion? 'What is [wrong] with you? How do you make judgement?'—what has happened to your intellect that you make such a twisted decision? 'Then will you not be reminded?'—meaning this statement of yours is a clear testimony that you do not utilize your senses at all; otherwise, how would you choose for God what you are not ready to choose for yourselves? The meaning is: this statement does not stand in need of any argument for proving its falsity, but only in need of 'Tadhakkur' (remembrance/reflection). If a person is not entirely devoid of intellect, they can understand this with minor attention.

"Or do you have a clear authority? Then bring your scripture, if you should be truthful." (156-157)

It states: if you have any clear proof for this, present it. The clear proof is explained here with the word 'Kitab' (scripture)—stating: if you have any testimony of any heavenly Book in favor of this claim of yours, present it. No one has the right to say anything about God according to their own assumption; rather, they have the right to say it only on the basis of proof, and the most clear proof can be His Book which He has sent down for the teaching and guidance of people.

"And they have claimed between Him and the jinn a lineage, but the jinn have already known that they will indeed be brought [into Hell], Exalted is Allah above what they describe, Except the chosen servants of Allah." (159-160)

Besides the Angels, They Have Also Associated the Jinn with God

In these verses, 'Exalted is Allah above what they describe' stands as a parenthetical clause, and 'Except the chosen servants of Allah' relates to 'but the jinn have already known that they will indeed be brought [into Hell]'. There are several parallels in the Qur'an showing that when some statement requires immediate refutation, the refutation comes in the middle of the actual sequence of the discourse. This expresses the reality that the statement is so disgusting that the speaker does not tolerate even that much delay as to let his statement be completed first.

The meaning of the verses is: these people have not committed only this excess of making the angels daughters of God, but have committed an even greater injustice of joining a relationship between the Jinn and God. The angels they associated in God's essence, and the Jinn they associated in God's rights and His attributes. Worship is specifically the right of Allah Almighty, but these unfortunate people believe the Jinn to be beneficial, harmful, and a source of obtaining the knowledge of the unseen, and on this illusion, they worship them and present sacrifices and offerings to them.

'but the jinn have already known that they will indeed be brought [into Hell]': This is exactly the case of the client being silent while the witness is active. The Jinn themselves know very well that they are God's creation and they must present themselves before their Lord one day to answer for their deeds, and those among them who are criminals will be punished. But these foolish people have assumed them to be partners in divinity and started worshipping them.

A Parenthetical Clause

'Exalted is Allah above what they describe': This statement—associating the Jinn with God—was highly disgusting; because of this, its refutation was made immediately—stating that the Exalted Essence of God is pure from all such relationships. No one is His partner in His essence or His attributes. He is entirely One and has no partner; therefore, He is also entirely One and has no partner in His rights.

'Except the chosen servants of Allah'—this exception is from 'indeed they will be brought [into Hell]'. Since 'muhdaroon' (brought) contains the meaning that they will be captured and brought like criminals, through this exception, He clarified that only those will escape from this whom Allah has chosen for His worship and obedience, and who remain safe from the trials of Satan.

It is unknown what confusion Al-Zamakhshari faced that he interpreted 'Jinn' as the angels here. We have explained the correct interpretation of the verses; therefore, there remains no need to refute his view. In the Qur'an, the angels and the Jinn are mentioned as two entirely different and differently characterized creations. There is no room to declare them as one. At most, what can be said is that the Jinn are closer to the angels than to humans in terms of their creation, because the Jinn were created from fire, the angels from light, and humans from dust.

"For indeed, you and what you worship, You cannot tempt [anyone] against Him, Except him who is to burn in Hell." (161-163)

Allah's Respite Is Only for Trial

Some have interpreted 'bi-fatineen' (cannot tempt) with the preposition 'ala' as 'against'. They consider the referent of the pronoun to be Allah Almighty. I do not possess full certainty on this, but if this explanation is accepted, these Jinn-worshippers are being warned: 'You and your deities, through this trial you have set up, can turn away from God only those who themselves are going to burn in Hell due to their bad deeds.' This is that very same point which Allah Almighty said in reply to Iblis's challenge—stating: 'Your power will operate only upon those who follow you; upon My sincere servants you will have no power,' expressed here in other words. The objective is to show that Allah Almighty has kept these trials in the system of this universe not to lead people into deviation, but merely to test them. Those servants of Allah who try to succeed in this test, Satan and his worshippers cannot harm them in the least.

The View of Quraishi Regarding the Referent of the Pronoun

According to the mentor-scholar (ra), the referent of the pronoun is 'ma ta'budoona' (what you worship). In his view, the interpretation is: 'You can make no one infatuated with your deities except him who himself intends to go to Hell.' I also do not possess full certainty on this interpretation. I have mentioned it only so that those of knowledge who wish to reflect on these verses also keep it before them.

The Angels' Declaration of Their True Position

Ayah 164-166

The statements made above regarding the status of the angels are from Allah Almighty. Now, in these verses, the chief of the angels, Hazrat Jibreel, directly clarifies his and all angels' status—stating what rank and station they hold before Allah Almighty, and in what works they are active in His presence. The objective is to humiliate those who were worshipping the angels, believing them to be partners of God—showing them what the chief of all angels says and what his foolish worshippers assume.

It should be kept in mind here that there are several clear examples in the Qur'an showing that within a discourse, some statement is directly made through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel. If a parallel is sought, have a look at verses 64-65 of Surah Maryam. This method is adopted in those places where making the statement through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel is more effective and eloquent. This is such an occasion. To strike at the illusion of those who believed the angels to be partners of God, the most effective thing was that Hazrat Jibreel himself clarified his and all angels' status. For this purpose, no testimony could be greater than Hazrat Jibreel's (peace be upon him) testimony. Now see what Hazrat Jibreel says.

"'And there is not among us any except that he has a known position.'" First of all, he says that every one of us has a specified sphere of work and his reach is limited. He says regarding himself as well that his reach also has a specified limit beyond which he cannot go. Have a look at what we wrote under verse 1 of Surah Fatir. This shows that the status of the angels is that of God's ruled and obedient servants. They can neither fly beyond their limits, nor interfere in any matter without God's permission, nor dare to do any work on their own. How foolish are those who believe these ruled servants of God to be partners in divinity, expecting that they can do whatever they please, to the extent that if God wishes to seize them, they will save them.

"'And indeed, we are those who stand in rows. And indeed, we are those who exalt [Allah].'" Second, he said that we are continuously standing in rows in His presence and continuously exalting Him. The standing of the angels in rows has been mentioned in the first verse of this Surah, and we have explained it. By 'Musabbihoon' (those who exalt), he meant that very same act which was described at the beginning of the Surah with the words: 'Taliyati Dhikra'. In our view, this is the angels' prayer around the Throne of God, which is the greatest manifest form of servitude. There it was described as 'Dhikr' (remembrance/message), here as 'Tasbeeh' (exaltation), and in the Qur'an, prayer is described by both of these words. In 'Tasbeeh', the aspect of declaring purity is dominant—meaning declaring God to be pure and high above all that is contrary to His majesty. By using this word, Hazrat Jibreel, as it were, clarified: 'Let alone being partners of God, we are continuously active in declaring God to be pure from all such things.'

"And indeed, they used to say, 'If we had with us a scripture from the former peoples, We would have been the devoted servants of Allah.' But they disbelieved in it, so they are going to know." (167-170)

A Claim of the Quraysh

This theme has passed in verse 42 of Surah Fatir, and we have explained it there. When the Quraysh heard that the Jews and other nations denied their respective prophets, they would say: 'How unfortunate were those who denied Allah's messengers; if we had any teaching of previous peoples, or if a messenger of Allah came to us, we would not adopt this attitude, but would follow his teaching and show ourselves to be the devoted servants of Allah.' A person always makes such claims regarding what they do not possess—stating that if they receive it, they will perform such achievements. But when they receive it, in most cases, they prove to be worse than their predecessors. Today, many people claim with great pomp that if they are given the reigns of government, they will show the world the glory of the Caliphate of Farooq anew; but when they are tested, at the very first step they prove to be highly incompetent, treacherous, and thieves. Very few individuals prove to be capable of this responsibility, and none among those who rise making tall claims of it turns out to be capable. The entire history of this world is witness to this reality.

'But they disbelieved in it, so they are going to know.' It states that when the very thing they desired and claimed arrived, they denied it, and are now fabricating various excuses for its denial; so they will soon know what end they will face for this denial of theirs.

This points to that end which the deniers of the messengers must face, and the objective is to warn the Quraysh—showing that if they do not change their way, the same Sunnah of Allah will appear in their case which appeared for previous deniers.

By 'Kalimah' (decree/word) here is meant that Sunnah of Allah which He has clarified in various places in the Qur'an regarding His messengers. For example:

'Allah has written, "I will surely overcome, I and My messengers." Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.' (Al-Mujadilah: 21).

'Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand' (Ghafir: 51).

We have been clarifying in various places in this book that the nation against whom Allah Almighty fully established proof through one of His messengers, if they did not believe, was destroyed. A messenger is the final means of the establishment of proof; for this reason, his advent holds the position of God's court for the nation. The conflict between truth and falsehood which is initiated through him ends with his and his companions' victory, and his opponents' destruction. We have explained all stages of this Sunnah in their proper places. Here, also keep in view the difference between a prophet (Nabi) and a messenger (Rasool) which we have explained.

'And indeed, Our soldiers will be those who overcome.' Along with the messenger and his companions, there is also the army of Allah's angels, and those who have the army of God's angels with them, no power of the world can defeat them. This army faces trials by way of test, but victory is ultimately achieved by it.

"So leave them for a time. And see [what will befall] them, for they are going to see." (174-175)

Instruction of Patience to the Prophet

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh) and instruct him to observe patience and wait. The meaning is: turn away from their offenses for a time, so that the proof of Allah is fully established against them and they fully fill their cup. Watch them for a time, they will soon see what they made and in what form its end appeared before them!

Ayah 176-179

That is: if, in case of denying the messenger, they hasten for the punishment of which they are being warned—saying that until that punishment or some sample of it is shown to them they will not believe, then inform them that the punishment is no ordinary thing. When it descends in their courtyards and fields, it will be a highly bad time for those who have been warned of it. The punishment that comes after the warning of the messengers completely destroys that nation.

It should be kept in mind here that in Arabia, the primary time for launching raids was the morning; for this reason, the word 'Sabah' (morning) is also used in the sense of a raid and an assault, and in the cry 'Wa-sabahah!', this is its meaning.

After this, the same theme of consolation and warning which passed in verses 174-175 above is repeated. The Prophet (pbuh) was consoled: ignore their nonsense and wait for a few more days; and the disbelievers were threatened: that time is soon coming when they will see with their own eyes everything of which they are being informed.

"Exalted is your Lord, the Lord of Might, above what they describe." (180)

That is, your Lord Who is the true Master of all might and power is high and pure above all those things which these polytheists attribute to Him. He informed them of these superstitions and warned them of their results. If they still do not desist, they will see their end.

"And peace upon the messengers." (181)

Just as this glad tidings of peace has passed above in connection with other messengers, similarly at the end this is glad tidings for the Messenger of Allah (pbuh)—showing that the Sunnah of Allah which appeared for all messengers is that which is for you. Upon Allah's messengers is peace. Destruction is for their enemies.

"And praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds." (182)

And Allah is worthy of praise and gratitude; therefore, in this conflict of truth and falsehood, He will necessarily elevate the truth and defeat falsehood. He is the Lord of the worlds; His lordship necessarily requires that His justice appears in this world as well, and His perfect justice also appears in the Hereafter. This is the requirement of His lordship, and on this basis, He is worthy of praise in both this world and the Hereafter.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.

Rahman Abad: 29 April, 1975 AD.
